chain_of_thought: Crash is in strpbrk, which is a standard C library function used to search a string for any of a set of bytes. Termination reason is just 'strpbrk', suggesting it crashed inside or immediately around it (likely null/invalid pointer or unterminated string).

Native stack: strpbrk -> decodeBytes -> Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse -> fuzz_one_input. App-native frame below libc is decodeBytes in libtbxml.so. I don't have decompiled native code due to tool errors, but I can reason about typical TBXML JNI implementations and the Java side we saw.

Java TBXML.parse(String) calls jniParse(str.getBytes()). So JNI gets a byte[] that is fully attacker-controlled (XML document string). No Java-side validation of content or length is done prior to JNI.

In native, TBXML libraries usually implement jniParse by turning the byte[] into a C string, then passing to an XML tokenizer. Function name decodeBytes strongly suggests it converts bytes to chars or decodes UTF-8, likely building a C string and then using strpbrk to look for delimiters (e.g., '<', '>', whitespace, etc.).

A crash in strpbrk normally occurs if the first argument is not a valid pointer to a NUL-terminated string, or if the second arg is invalid. Both are under the library’s control; but the *contents* and length of the byte array originate from untrusted input via Java. If decodeBytes fails to ensure the buffer is null-terminated or reads beyond bounds while scanning for terminator, it can pass a non-terminated or freed buffer into strpbrk, causing out-of-bounds read and crash. That is clear memory-safety bug in libtbxml, not just harness-only misuse.

The fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary bytes into TBXML.parse equivalent. However, in a real app, the same jniParse is used for any XML document string; attacker could supply overlong or malformed XML (from network or file) to trigger the same path. There is no evidence that only the fuzzer passes impossible states (e.g., calling jniParse with a null jbyteArray); Java TBXML.parse always passes str.getBytes() which is non-null. Fuzzer could also be using the same JNI directly; still realistic if app exposes that path to user-controlled XML.

We don't see explicit memcpy/memmove in stack, but strpbrk itself scans memory; if decodeBytes calculated an incorrect length, or mis-handled encoding, that can yield out-of-bounds read. That is a likely vulnerability (CWE-125 or CWE-787) because it is memory-corruption-prone and attacker-controlled input influences the scanned buffer. Severity is high because it’s native parsing of untrusted XML. We lack exact C code, but call structure and crash location give strong evidence of a real bug rather than harness/environment-only issue.

Therefore, classify as vulnerable, high severity, with theoretical-to-practical exploitability: crafted XML bytes passed to TBXML.parse can cause DoS and potentially further memory corruption depending on allocator and platform. Provide generic PoC using the TBXML.parse(String) call via app Activities or test harness, with assumptions noted.
reasons: Crash occurs inside libc strpbrk called from libtbxml.so:decodeBytes, not in harness-only code
Java TBXML.parse passes attacker-controlled bytes from String directly to JNI jniParse without validation
decodeBytes likely constructs/uses a C string from these bytes and calls strpbrk; a crash there indicates misuse of string pointers or termination (out-of-bounds read)
Same JNI entry is used in production (TBXML.parse), so malformed XML input from realistic sources could trigger the same native bug
recommendations: Review and harden decodeBytes implementation to ensure all buffers passed to strpbrk are valid, in-bounds, and NUL-terminated
Add explicit length tracking and bounds checks when converting Java byte[] to native strings instead of relying on implicit termination
Add fuzzing and ASan/Valgrind runs on libtbxml with varied XML inputs to locate and fix all string-scanning related issues
assumptions: decodeBytes converts the Java byte[] into a C string and calls strpbrk on it
Java TBXML.parse or similar entrypoints allow attacker-controlled XML strings in realistic scenarios (e.g., network or file input)
The crash is due to invalid pointer or unterminated string passed into strpbrk, not a glibc bug
limitations: No decompiled native code for decodeBytes or jniParse was available to confirm exact buffer handling and bounds checks
Exact crash condition (null pointer vs out-of-bounds read) is not shown in the provided trace
No full Java call graph to confirm how untrusted inputs reach TBXML.parse in this specific app

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a specially crafted XML string that is parsed via TBXML.parse, leading to invalid buffer state in decodeBytes and a crash in strpbrk
prerequisites: Attacker can influence or supply XML content processed by TBXML.parse (e.g., via network, file, or IPC)
Application uses TBXML.parse on attacker-controlled data
exploit_pipeline: Craft an overlong or malformed XML string designed to stress TBXML’s byte decoding (e.g., invalid encodings, missing terminators)
Deliver this string to the application through a feature that calls TBXML.parse (such as file import, network response parsing, or test harness)
Application forwards the bytes into native jniParse, which invokes decodeBytes
decodeBytes mis-handles the byte buffer and calls strpbrk with an invalid or unterminated C string, causing out-of-bounds read and process crash
Iterate on payload to look for more severe memory-corruption symptoms beyond simple crash (e.g., with ASan or heap grooming)
poc_commands: # Alternatively, invoke instrumentation test that calls new TBXML().parse(craftedXmlString) with the payload string
exploit.notes: Exact XML structure that triggers the crash is unknown; fuzzer input should be minimized to derive a concrete PoC document. Replace <package>/<activity> with real component names that ultimately call TBXML.parse on attacker-supplied data.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in strchr_default, called from decodeBytes in libtbxml.so, then from JNI bridge Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse, then fuzz_one_input/main.

strchr_default suggests the C library strchr was invoked with a bad pointer (e.g., invalid/misaligned address) or led to an abort due to UBSan/Scudo; but we have no sanitizer frames indicated, only process termination label 'strchr_default'.

decodeBytes is the first app-level frame; however Ghidra MCP search/decompile for decodeBytes and the JNI method failed with HTTP 404, so their implementations and argument flows cannot be inspected. We therefore cannot confirm exact operations, pointer arithmetic, or bounds checks.

Given the method names and library (TBXML XML parser), decodeBytes likely converts a Java byte[] (XML data) into an internal representation and uses strchr to search for delimiters in that data without an explicit length, assuming a NUL-terminated C string.

The fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input) almost certainly passes arbitrary byte buffers that may not contain any NUL byte and may not be NUL-terminated in native space. If decodeBytes treats this buffer as a C string and calls strchr, the search may scan past allocated bounds, causing invalid memory access and crash.

In a real app, XML input ultimately comes from external sources (network/file/IPC) and reaches the JNI parser. If the native layer does not enforce a length-bounded search (e.g., memchr with supplied size, or prior NUL-termination in a properly allocated buffer), malformed or extremely large inputs could similarly cause out-of-bounds read and a crash (DoS) or, in some environments, exploitable memory corruption.

However, without decompiled code, we cannot see whether JNI or decodeBytes copy the Java byte[] into a separately allocated and always NUL-terminated buffer before calling strchr, which would turn the crash into a harness artifact (e.g., harness passing a non-Java-like buffer or violating expected invariants).

Given typical TBXML-style parsers, unsafe C-string assumptions on attacker-controlled XML are a common pattern, and the function name decodeBytes suggests direct use of the inbound data. The fuzz harness also appears simple: fuzz_one_input -> JNI -> decodeBytes, which matches a realistic app path, not an artificial one that forces impossible invariants.

Balancing uncertainty (no code view) against domain knowledge (C XML parser using strchr on untrusted bytes), this looks like a likely out-of-bounds read on attacker-controlled input, at least leading to denial-of-service and potentially memory corruption depending on allocator and surrounding code.

Thus, I classify it as a likely vulnerability with moderate confidence, mapping to CWE-125 (out-of-bounds read) / CWE-787 (if writes or allocator interaction are involved) but focusing on OOB read/DoS impact due to lack of concrete write evidence.
reasons: Crash occurs in C library strchr, called from app function decodeBytes within libtbxml.so, indicating misuse of C-string APIs on native buffers.
Input path fuzz_one_input -> Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse -> decodeBytes matches realistic XML parsing from attacker-controllable data (network/file).
Function naming and typical TBXML-style parsers strongly suggest unbounded strchr over untrusted bytes, risking out-of-bounds read and process crash.
Lack of visible validation or length-bounded search between JNI and decodeBytes leaves attacker influence on the buffer unchecked.
recommendations: Inspect and decompile decodeBytes and the JNI bridge to confirm how the input byte[] is handled before strchr is called.
Replace strchr with a length-bounded variant (e.g., memchr with explicit size) or ensure the buffer is copied into a properly sized, NUL-terminated allocation before C-string operations.
Add robust bounds checking and input size limits in both Java and JNI layers when passing XML or arbitrary bytes into TBXML parsing routines.
assumptions: Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse receives attacker-controlled bytes (e.g., XML from network, files, or IPC).
decodeBytes passes a pointer into this buffer directly to strchr without first guaranteeing NUL-termination within the allocated range.
The fuzz harness does not intentionally violate invariants that production Java/JNI code would always maintain.
limitations: Unable to decompile decodeBytes or the JNI bridge due to tooling HTTP errors, so pointer and length handling is inferred, not observed.
No detailed crash context (registers, faulting address, or sanitizer logs) to distinguish between generic abort vs. concrete out-of-bounds read.
No Java-side call graph details beyond the JNI method name, limiting analysis of higher-level validation and size checks.

exploit.trigger_method: malformed or unusually large XML/byte payload parsed via TBXML JNI
prerequisites: Ability to supply arbitrary or large XML/byte content to the TBXML parser from network, file, or IPC.
App must invoke TBXML.jniParse (or equivalent) on untrusted data without additional sanitization.
Native library compiled without full hardening that would fully mask or convert OOB reads into harmless aborts.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an XML or binary payload that lacks NUL terminators within the expected range or that is extremely large, maximizing the scan range for strchr in decodeBytes.
Deliver this payload to the vulnerable code path (e.g., via a file opened in the app or an API that passes the content to TBXML.jniParse).
Trigger the TBXML parsing operation so that Java_za_co_twyst_tbxml_TBXML_jniParse calls into decodeBytes with the crafted buffer.

When decodeBytes calls strchr on the unbounded buffer, the search runs past allocated memory, causing an out-of-bounds read and process termination (denial-of-service) and potentially exposing memory or enabling further memory corruption depending on allocator behavior.
exploit.notes: The PoC assumes there is an app path that loads an external XML file and passes its contents into TBXML.jniParse. Exact activity and intent extras need to be filled in once the Java call graph is inspected.
